content_id:
Empat khalifah rasyid — Abu Bakr al-Shiddiq, 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, 'Utsman ibn 'Affan, dan 'Ali ibn Abi Thalib — memerintah komunitas Muslim dari 11 H hingga 40 H, sebuah periode ekspansi luar biasa dan tantangan internal yang intens. Kisah Ibn al-Atsir tentang era ini mengandalkan sumber-sumber awal utama termasuk al-Thabari, Thabaqat Ibn Sa'd, dan kisah-kisah dari para Sahabat agung, menyajikan narasi yang sekaligus terperinci dan terorganisasi dengan baik. Beliau membahas Perang-Perang Kemurtadan di bawah Abu Bakr, pembukaan Irak dan Syam di bawah baik Abu Bakr maupun 'Umar, dan kemenangan-kemenangan besar di Ajnadain, al-Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, dan Nahawand yang membawa jantung-jantung Bizantium dan Sassanid di bawah pemerintahan Islam dalam satu generasi.

Kekhalifahan 'Umar ibn al-Khattab mendapat perlakuan yang sangat penuh, mencerminkan kepentingan historisnya sebagai organisator besar negara Islam. Ibn al-Atsir mendokumentasikan inovasi-inovasi administratif 'Umar: pembentukan diwan, penetapan kalender hijriah, institusi tunjangan tetap bagi para prajurit, pengangkatan gubernur-gubernur dan hakim-hakim, dan survei serta pemukiman di wilayah-wilayah yang baru ditaklukkan. Kesederhanaan pribadi 'Umar, aksesibilitasnya bagi kaum Muslim biasa, akuntabilitas gubernur-gubernurnya, dan komitmennya terhadap keadilan bahkan dengan biaya pribadi merupakan tema-tema berulang yang Ibn al-Atsir dokumentasikan dengan anekdot-anekdot spesifik yang diambil dari tradisi historis.

Fitnah — huru-hara sipil besar pertama — yang meletus selama kekhalifahan 'Utsman dan berlanjut melalui kekhalifahan 'Ali diperlakukan dengan keseimbangan yang menjadi ciri khas historiografi Sunni klasik. Ibn al-Atsir mencatat keluhan-keluhan yang mendorong oposisi terhadap 'Utsman, keadaan-keadaan kemartiran beliau, posisi kompleks dari para Sahabat senior yang menolak untuk berpartisipasi dalam konflik, dan pertempuran-pertempuran Jamal serta Shiffin dengan konsekuensi-konsekuensi tragisnya. Beliau menghindari polemik-polemik sektarian dari mereka yang mengecam 'Ali maupun mereka yang mengecam para lawannya, menyajikan para Sahabat sebagai manusia-manusia yang bertindak di bawah tekanan yang luar biasa sementara mempertahankan rasa hormatnya terhadap kebajikan dan status keseluruhan mereka.

Kisah Ibn al-Atsir tentang khilafah rasyidah berakhir dengan pembunuhan 'Ali pada 40 H dan kekhalifahan singkat al-Hasan ibn 'Ali, yang melepaskan klaimnya kepada Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan untuk mengakhiri pertumpahan darah di antara kaum Muslim. Tindakan pengorbanan ini, yang dilaporkan Nabi telah ramalkan dan puji, mengakhiri era Khulafaur Rasyidin dan membuka periode Umayyah. Narasi Ibn al-Atsir menyampaikan baik pencapaian-pencapaian luar biasa maupun tragedi yang mendalam dari era fondasi ini, yang peristiwa-peristinya terus membentuk perdebatan-perdebatan politik dan teologis Islam selama berabad-abad.
content_en:
The four rightly-guided caliphs — Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, 'Uthman ibn 'Affan, and 'Ali ibn Abi Talib — governed the Muslim community from 11 AH to 40 AH, a period of extraordinary expansion and intense internal challenge. Ibn al-Athir's account of this era draws on the major early sources including al-Tabari, Ibn Sa'd's Tabaqat, and the accounts of the great Companions, presenting a narrative that is both detailed and carefully organized. He covers the Wars of Apostasy under Abu Bakr, the opening of Iraq and Syria under both Abu Bakr and 'Umar, and the great victories at Ajnadayn, al-Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, and Nahawand that brought the Byzantine and Sassanid heartlands under Islamic rule within a single generation.

The caliphate of 'Umar ibn al-Khattab receives especially full treatment, reflecting his historical importance as the great organizer of the Islamic state. Ibn al-Athir documents 'Umar's administrative innovations: the establishment of the diwan, the fixing of the hijri calendar, the institution of regular stipends for the soldiers, the appointment of governors and judges, and the survey and settlement of the newly conquered territories. 'Umar's personal austerity, his accessibility to ordinary Muslims, his accountability of governors, and his commitment to justice even at personal cost are recurring themes that Ibn al-Athir documents with specific anecdotes drawn from the historical tradition.

The fitna — the first great civil strife — that erupted during the caliphate of 'Uthman and continued through the caliphate of 'Ali is treated with the careful balance that characterizes classical Sunni historiography. Ibn al-Athir notes the grievances that fueled the opposition to 'Uthman, the circumstances of his martyrdom, the complex position of the senior Companions who refused to participate in the conflict, and the battles of the Camel and Siffin with their tragic consequences. He avoids the sectarian polemics of both those who condemn 'Ali and those who condemn his opponents, presenting the Companions as human beings acting under extraordinary pressure while maintaining his respect for their overall virtue and status.

Ibn al-Athir's account of the rightly-guided caliphate ends with the assassination of 'Ali in 40 AH and the brief caliphate of al-Hasan ibn 'Ali, who relinquished his claim to Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan in order to end the bloodshed among Muslims. This act of sacrifice, which the Prophet had reportedly predicted and praised, brings the era of the rightly-guided caliphs to a close and opens the Umayyad period. Ibn al-Athir's narrative conveys both the extraordinary achievements and the profound tragedy of this founding era, whose events continued to shape Islamic political and theological debates for centuries.
